subject: ap-calculus-ab
title: Water Temperature Table W(t) — Rate Approx, FTC Integral, Left Riemann Avg & Model Value
stimulus:
The temperature of water in a tub at time $t$ is modeled by a strictly increasing, twice-differentiable function $W$, where $W(t)$ is measured in degrees Fahrenheit and $t$ is measured in minutes. At time $t = 0$, the temperature of the water is 55°F. The water is heated for 30 minutes, beginning at time $t = 0$. Values of $W(t)$ at selected times $t$ for the first 20 minutes are given in the table.

| $t$ (minutes) | 0 | 4 | 9 | 15 | 20 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $W(t)$ (°F) | 55.0 | 57.1 | 61.8 | 67.9 | 71.0 |

parts.prompt: Use the data in the table to estimate $W'(12)$. Show the computations that lead to your answer. Using correct units, interpret the meaning of your answer in the context of this problem.
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: $W'(12) \approx \frac{67.9-61.8}{6} \approx 1.017$ °F/min. Interpretation must include rate, direction, units, and context.

scoring_points.description: Interpretation with units: the water temperature is increasing at a rate of approximately $1.017$ °F per minute at time $t = 12$ minutes

required_elements: Interpretation with units: the water temperature is increasing at a rate of approximately $1.017$ °F per minute at time $t = 12$ minutes

parts.prompt: Use the data in the table to evaluate $\int_0^{20} W'(t)\, dt$. Using correct units, interpret the meaning of $\int_0^{20} W'(t)\, dt$ in the context of this problem.
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: By FTC, $\int_0^{20} W'(t)\, dt = W(20) - W(0) = 16$ °F. Interpretation: water warmed 16°F over 20 minutes.

alternatives.correct_example: $\int_0^{20} W'(t)\, dt = W(20) - W(0) = 71.0 - 55.0 = 16$ degrees Fahrenheit

scoring_points.description: Interpretation with units: the water has warmed by 16°F over the interval from $t = 0$ to $t = 20$ minutes

required_elements: Interpretation with units: the water has warmed by 16°F over the interval from $t = 0$ to $t = 20$ minutes

parts.prompt: For $0 \le t \le 20$, the average temperature of the water in the tub is $\frac{1}{20}\int_0^{20} W(t)\, dt$. Use a left Riemann sum with the four subintervals indicated by the data in the table to approximate $\frac{1}{20}\int_0^{20} W(t)\, dt$. Does this approximation overestimate or underestimate the average temperature of the water over these 20 minutes? Explain your reasoning.
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: Left Riemann sum $= \frac{1}{20}(220 + 285.5 + 370.8 + 339.5) = \frac{1215.8}{20} = 60.79$ °F. Underestimate because $W$ is strictly increasing.

scoring_points.description: Underestimate with reason: a left Riemann sum is used and $W$ is strictly increasing, so left endpoints give values below the function over each subinterval

required_elements: Underestimate with reason: a left Riemann sum is used
$W$ is strictly increasing, so left endpoints give values below the function over each subinterval

parts.prompt: For $20 \le t \le 25$, the function $W$ that models the water temperature has first derivative given by $W'(t) = 0.4\sqrt{t}\cos(0.06t)$. Based on the model, what is the temperature of the water at time $t = 25$?
